title: Open descendent integrals in arbitrary genus
speaker: Jake Solomon
abstract: Abstract: I plan to discuss the definition of open descendent integrals. In genus g>0, this involves the moduli space of Riemann surfaces with boundary with an additional structure called a grading. *Joint work with R. Tessler.

proof_stages.label: Research target and motivation for open descendants
proof_stages.summary: The talk situates open descendant integrals as an open/bordered analogue of closed psi-class integrals and Witten--Kontsevich theory, announces an in-progress higher-genus construction, and motivates the theory through combinatorial/matrix models and Fukaya-categorical applications.

proof_stages.label: Genus-zero moduli spaces and tautological lines
proof_stages.summary: The genus-zero spaces M_{0,k,l} of stable disks with k boundary and l interior marked points are defined, their dimension k+2l-3 is recalled, and tautological cotangent line bundles at interior markings are introduced as the intended sources of psi-classes.

proof_stages.label: Relative-cohomology obstruction on manifolds with corners
proof_stages.summary: The naive integrals of products of first Chern classes are shown not to be directly defined because the moduli spaces have genuine codimension-one boundary, so absolute cohomology classes must be lifted to relative cohomology via boundary data, for which no natural line-bundle trivialization is available.

proof_stages.label: Euler-class reformulation using a boundary section
proof_stages.summary: The product of psi-classes is replaced by the Euler class of a direct-sum bundle E=\bigoplus_i L_i^{\oplus a_i}, reducing the problem to choosing a canonical nowhere-vanishing section of E on the boundary and defining a relative Euler class by intersecting an extension with the zero section.

proof_stages.label: Canonical boundary condition from factorization and forgetting nodes
proof_stages.summary: Boundary components are factored as products of lower-dimensional disk moduli spaces, parity of the number of boundary markings selects an odd side on which a node can be forgotten without destabilizing relevant components, and this yields pullback identifications of E that define what it means for the boundary section to be canonical.

proof_stages.label: Existence and uniqueness of canonical multisections
proof_stages.summary: A theorem is stated that canonical multisections exist and are unique up to nowhere-vanishing homotopy; multivaluedness is needed because of monodromy around boundary faces, and existence/uniqueness are justified by an induction over strata together with rank-versus-dimension estimates.

examples.name: Fukaya algebra of real projective space
examples.note: The Fukaya A-infinity algebra of RP^{2n}, and the resulting Welschinger-type real enumerative invariants, are cited as an application showing why open descendant integrals matter beyond the point-target theory.
examples.engagement_reason: the Fukaya algebra of real projective space is cited as motivation via known/proposed formulas for structure coefficients, but no calculation is carried out on it

passes.reason: RP^n is a highly symmetric homogeneous Lagrangian, cited as a motivating application rather than as a refutation.
the Fukaya algebra is attached to the standard homogeneous real projective Lagrangian, used as motivation rather than to refute a claim

examples.name: Interior marked point hitting the boundary
examples.note: A disk degeneration in which an interior marked point reaches the boundary, possibly bubbling off with boundary marked points, illustrates why the boundary of the moduli space is subtler than the boundary of the source disk and why boundary trivializations are noncanonical.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes the boundary stratum where an interior point hits the disk boundary and, in the M_{0,3,1} model, applies the forgetful construction and tracks the section along that edge

passes.reason: An interior marking reaching the disk boundary is a boundary degeneration of the stable-disk moduli space.

examples.name: Two interior points colliding at the boundary
examples.note: The discussed degeneration with two interior marked points running into one another and toward the boundary illustrates additional twisting/monodromy difficulties for trivializing tautological line bundles on boundary strata.
examples.engagement_reason: two interior points colliding and hitting the boundary is raised only as a possible harder boundary phenomenon, without a construction or computation on it

passes.reason: The simultaneous collision at the boundary is a deeper boundary degeneration used as an obstruction to naive trivialization choices.

examples.name: Five boundary points and one interior point monodromy
examples.note: The speaker mentions M_{0,5,1} as a concrete case where moving around ten boundary faces forces a three-branched multisection, illustrating why honest single-valued sections are insufficient.
examples.engagement_reason: the five-boundary-one-interior monodromy example is described as having three branches and ten faces, but the speaker explicitly says there is no time to work it out

passes.reason: The loop through boundary faces in the five-boundary-one-interior case gives nontrivial monodromy, obstructing a single-valued section.
the five-boundary-one-interior monodromy shows a single-valued canonical section fails and at least three branches are needed

examples.name: Hexagon model for M_{0,3,1}
examples.note: The moduli space M_{0,3,1} is drawn as a hexagon, and the winding of the canonical boundary section is used to illustrate the relative Euler-class definition and the resulting open descendant integral \int e(L_1)=2.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker computes the dimension of M_{0,3,1}, identifies it as a hexagon, describes its boundary strata, tracks section twisting, and derives the integral value

passes.reason: The M_{0,3,1} hexagon is an ordinary low-dimensional moduli model whose computed twisting shows the unmodified Deligne equation expectation fails.
the compactified moduli space is modeled by a regular hexagon, and its computation shows the naive Deligne-equation expectation must be modified
